Public Involvement, Notification, and Assessment

We have LEA official(s)/designee(s) in charge of wellness policy
compliance.
Name(s)/Title(s):

We complete an assessment of the local school wellness policy at least
every three years (“triennial assessment”).
Triennial assessment results are made available to the public in an easily
accessible manner.
Website address and/or description of how to access copy:

At least every three years we use the results of the triennial assessment to
update or modify the wellness policy as needed.
The LEA informs and updates the public about the contents, updates, and
implementation of the wellness policy at least annually and the policy is
accessible to the public.
Website address for policy and/or description of how to access copy:

We retain records as required by federal regulations including:
□ The written school wellness policy,
□ Documentation of making the wellness policy publicly available,
□ Documentation of outreach efforts inviting stakeholders to participate in the
  wellness committee / wellness policy process, and
□ Copy of triennial assessment and documentation of reporting results to public.

The LEA utilizes a wellness committee that includes these community
stakeholders in the development, implementation, review, and update of the
wellness policy:
□ Administrators      □ Food service staff      □ School health professionals
□ Parents             □ School board members    □ PE teachers    □ Students
□ Public
Other stakeholders (describe):


Notes on public involvement, notification, and assessment:



Nutrition Education*

Nutrition education is provided within PDE’s sequential, comprehensive health
education standards.
We teach, model, encourage, and support healthy eating through nutrition
education.

Additional stakeholders regularly involved in the wellness committee include:

- Community Health Partners:
  - A representative from North Hennepin Community Health Clinic (currently: Olivia Tran, MPH, Community Health Liaison), who provides data on student health trends (e.g., BMI screening summaries, immunization rates) and assists with aligning school wellness efforts to county health initiatives.

- Mental Health Providers:
  - A licensed school social worker (currently: Anthony Miller, MSW, LGSW) and a contracted school-based therapist from Lakeside Counseling (currently: Sarah Nguyen, LMFT). They contribute perspectives on student stress, anxiety, and the impact of academic pressure on wellness, and help integrate mental wellness strategies into the policy.

- After-School Program Coordinators:
  - The district’s Community Education Coordinator (currently: Rebecca “Becky” Thompson) and a representative from the local YMCA youth sports program (currently: Daniel Ortiz). They ensure that physical activity and nutrition standards extend into after-school programming, including snack guidelines and screen-time limits.

- Union/Staff Association Representatives:
  - One representative from the teachers’ association and one from the classified staff association (current teacher rep: Linda Park, 8th Grade Science Teacher; current classified rep: Mark Ellis, Head Custodian). They provide feedback about feasibility of implementation, staff wellness initiatives, and how wellness activities fit into the school day and staff workload.

- Special Education Representative:
  - A special education case manager (currently: Hannah Williams, MA, Special Education Teacher) participates to ensure that wellness initiatives are inclusive and accessible for students with disabilities, including adaptive physical education options and modified meal accommodations.

- Parent-Teacher Organization (PTO/PTA) Representative:
  - The current PTO president or designee (for 2025–2026: Maria Gonzalez). This representative helps coordinate wellness messaging at PTO events, supports healthy fundraising alternatives, and assists with parent outreach for family wellness nights.

- Student Club Advisors:
  - The advisor for the Students for Healthy Living Club (currently: Mr. Patrick O’Connor, PE Department Chair) and the advisor for the Garden & Sustainability Club (currently: Ms. Jenna Lee, RN, School Nurse). They help coordinate student-led initiatives, such as the school garden, hydration campaigns, and social media wellness challenges.

answers.field_label: Notes On Public Involvement Notification And Assessment
answers.value:
The Maple Grove Unified School District has made a sustained effort over the last three years to broaden and deepen public involvement in the wellness policy process, particularly by ensuring that families from diverse linguistic and cultural backgrounds are aware of opportunities to participate.

Meeting Frequency and Format:
- The District Wellness Committee meets at least quarterly (September, December, March, and May), with additional working sessions scheduled as needed when policy revisions or new initiatives are being considered.
- Meetings are held in the early evening (typically 6:00–7:30 PM) at the District Office or the Maple Grove Middle-High School library to accommodate working families.
- A virtual attendance option (via Zoom or Google Meet) is consistently offered, which has significantly increased participation from parents who cannot attend in person due to child care, transportation, or work schedules.

Notification Methods:
- Meeting notices and invitations to participate are sent at least two weeks in advance through multiple channels, including:
  - District-wide email announcements via the SchoolMessenger system (English, Spanish, and Somali).
  - Posts on the district website under the “News & Announcements” section.
  - Flyers sent home with students at the start of the school year and again in January, outlining the annual schedule of wellness committee meetings.
  - Social media posts on the district’s official Facebook and Twitter/X accounts.
  - Announcements in each school’s weekly electronic newsletter, with a brief description of the topics to be discussed (e.g., “reviewing cafeteria menu changes,” “discussing mental health supports,” etc.).

Examples of Public Input:
- In fall 2024, a group of parents expressed concerns about the amount of time students spent waiting in cafeteria lines, which reduced their actual eating time. As a result, the committee worked with Food Service and building administrators to pilot staggered lunch schedules and an additional mobile “grab-and-go” line at the high school. Follow-up surveys showed improved student satisfaction and fewer reports of students skipping lunch due to long lines.
- Student representatives on the committee advocated for more vegetarian and culturally diverse menu options. In response, the district added a rotating menu of vegetarian entrees (such as black bean tacos, hummus and veggie wraps, and vegetable stir-fry) and introduced pilot “Cultural Heritage Days” featuring dishes inspired by student and family recipes (e.g., pozole, chicken biryani, and lentil stew), while still meeting USDA meal pattern requirements.
- Several parents and a school nurse raised concerns about energy drinks and highly caffeinated beverages being brought to school. The committee recommended stronger enforcement of existing rules prohibiting energy drinks on campus and collaborated with health teachers to incorporate lessons on caffeine, sleep hygiene, and healthy hydration. The district also expanded its water bottle filling stations from 6 to 14 locations across the middle-high school campus.

Documentation Practices:
- Sign-in sheets, meeting agendas, minutes, and presentation materials are saved in a shared district Google Drive folder managed by the Director of Student Wellness.
- A brief summary of key decisions and next steps is posted on the district website after each quarterly meeting, along with a link for stakeholders to submit additional comments or suggestions via an online form.
- The district maintains copies of all email blasts, website screenshots, and flyers used to promote wellness committee meetings and triennial assessment results, in order to document outreach efforts for state and federal program reviews.

Efforts to Increase Diverse Representation:
- Recognizing that earlier wellness committees did not fully reflect the diversity of the student population, the district has intentionally recruited parents and students from underrepresented groups. For example, the district partnered with the Latino Parent Advisory Council and the Somali Parent Network to identify parent volunteers willing to serve on the committee.
- The district provides interpreters for Spanish and Somali at wellness committee meetings when requested, and has translated key materials (e.g., wellness policy summary, meeting invitations) into these languages.
- Child care is offered during in-person evening meetings (supervised by high school volunteers earning community service hours), which has reduced barriers for families with young children.

Continuous Improvement:
- Based on feedback from a 2025 stakeholder survey, the committee plans to create a concise, family-friendly “Wellness Policy at a Glance” brochure and short video overview to make the policy more accessible and understandable.
- The committee is also exploring the development of a student-led wellness advisory group at each secondary school to provide more direct input on day-to-day implementation issues, such as healthy vending, water access, and mental health awareness campaigns.

Overall, while the district views its current level of public involvement as a strength, the committee acknowledges that participation still tends to be higher among families who are already engaged with the school system. Increasing awareness and participation among less-engaged families, including those facing economic or language barriers, remains a priority for the next triennial cycle.

persona.background_context:
This is Melissa, a mid-career educator who has gradually become the point person for anything related to student wellness in Maple Grove Unified School District. She and her family live in a modest but comfortable split-level home about ten minutes from the Maple Grove Middle-High School Campus, where both of her kids go to school. Weekdays start early: she’s usually up by 5:45 a.m., squeezing in a 25-minute treadmill walk in the basement while skimming overnight emails on her phone. By 6:30 she’s nudging her 11-year-old son Jake out of bed and trying to negotiate with her 14-year-old daughter Ellie about screen time and outfit choices. Her husband Daniel handles breakfast—often scrambled eggs or oatmeal—while Melissa packs her laptop bag and reviews her calendar, mentally triaging which meetings can realistically run short and which will inevitably run long.

Professionally, Melissa is in the thick of a busy stretch. As Director of Student Wellness & Compliance, she coordinates the district’s wellness initiatives and works closely with colleagues like Carlos Ramirez, the Food Service Coordinator; Jenna Lee, the district’s school nurse coordinator; and Patrick O’Connor, the PE Department Chair. Over the past couple of years, she’s invested a lot of energy into making wellness efforts more visible and accessible to families—pushing for multi-language communication, hybrid evening meetings, and genuine student input. A typical day might include a morning check-in with Carlos about menu changes and cafeteria line issues, a midday meeting with school administrators about enforcing the energy drink rules, and an afternoon planning session with Jenna and Patrick about expanding mental health and hydration campaigns. She’s proud of the new mobile “grab-and-go” lunch line and the additional water bottle filling stations on the middle-high school campus, but she still worries about students who skip meals or rely on convenience store snacks before school.

At home, life feels like a constant balancing act. Ellie, now a high school freshman, is juggling honors English, JV soccer, and the Students for Healthy Living club that Patrick advises. She’s starting to test boundaries—staying up late on group chats, pushing for more independence, and asking about part-time work next summer. Melissa is both proud and anxious, trying to support Ellie’s growing independence while keeping an eye on sleep, stress, and social media drama. Jake, in 6th grade, is adjusting to the bigger campus and rotating schedule. He loves the new Cultural Heritage Days in the cafeteria—his current favorite lunch is the lentil stew—and has recently joined an after-school robotics club. He struggles a bit with organization, so Melissa and Daniel have set up a shared family calendar and a Sunday-night backpack check ritual that’s become part argument, part bonding.

Extended family is a quiet but steady presence in Melissa’s life. Her parents, Maggie and Robert, still live in Bloomington and help with occasional weekend child care, especially during soccer tournaments or late evening school events. Melissa’s mother-in-law, Karen, moved into an assisted living facility in nearby Plymouth last year after a series of falls. Melissa visits her every other Sunday with the kids, which has added another regular commitment to an already full calendar but also gives her children a tangible sense of family continuity. Over the past few months, Melissa has been feeling the strain of being in the “sandwich generation”—fielding calls from Karen’s nurses about medication changes in between back-to-back meetings about student stress and anxiety.

Her main personal concern lately is sustainability—both her own and the district’s. She’s been sleeping less than she should and often ends up finishing reports or presentations from the couch after the kids go to bed. A mild bout of stress-related migraines last fall was a wake-up call; since then, she’s tried to protect at least one weeknight as a no-meeting, no-laptop evening for the family to eat dinner together and watch a show or play a board game. On those nights, she and Daniel enforce a “phones in the basket” rule after 7:30 p.m., which Ellie complains about but mostly goes along with. On weekends, Melissa makes a point of walking the neighborhood trails or visiting the local YMCA, sometimes coordinating with community partners like Daniel Ortiz from the YMCA youth sports program, blurring the lines between her personal and professional worlds.

Looking ahead, Melissa is focused on two big projects. First, she’s working with a small team—including Jenna, Carlos, and parent representative Maria Gonzalez—to create a concise, family-friendly “Wellness Policy at a Glance” brochure and a short video overview that will be easier for busy families to digest than the full policy documents. Second, she’s excited about piloting student-led wellness advisory groups at the secondary schools, believing strongly that students themselves should help shape policies on things like vending machines, water access, and mental health awareness campaigns. She knows that not all families feel comfortable engaging with the school system, especially those facing language or economic barriers, and this weighs on her. In quiet moments—usually late at night in the kitchen with a cup of herbal tea—she thinks about how to reach those families more effectively, so that the wellness work she cares about doesn’t just benefit the students who are already most connected and outspoken.

form_summary.form_purpose: Assessment and reporting tool for a school district or LEA to document the existence, implementation status, and compliance of its local school wellness policy, particularly regarding public involvement, notification, assessment, and nutrition education.
form_summary.intended_recipient: District/LEA administrators and wellness committee members completing compliance reviews, as well as state or federal education/child nutrition program officials who monitor and verify adherence to wellness policy regulations.

question_secrets.question_text: Website Address And Or Description Of How To Access Copy Triennial Assessment Results

secrets.detail_content: The full triennial assessment results are posted on the Maple Grove Unified School District website at https://www.maplegroveusd.org/departments/wellness/triennial-assessment, but because Karen Hart’s dementia has progressed since her fall in March 2023 at the Plymouth assisted living facility, I’ve also been keeping a printed copy at our home at 1200 Oakwood Drive, Maple Grove, MN 55369 so I can go over the nutrition and physical activity sections with her during my Sunday visits and help her understand what Ellie and Jake are eating at school.
secrets.why_sensitive: This overshares a family member’s specific medical condition (dementia), a recent injury, and care arrangements that have nothing to do with the district’s compliance documentation. Disclosing a relative’s health status and living situation to state or federal reviewers in a wellness-policy form is unnecessary, could violate Karen’s privacy, and could invite bias or inappropriate curiosity about the family’s circumstances.

It exposes internal personnel matters in a compliance form meant for policy documentation, not HR disputes.

artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

Finally sitting down after getting Ellie to stop doomscrolling TikTok and Jake to finish his robotics homework. I wanted to catch you up on a few things, since you always get the unfiltered version.

First, Karen. Since her fall back in March 2023 at the Plymouth assisted living place and the dementia diagnosis that followed, she’s been more anxious about what the kids are eating. I’ve started printing the full triennial wellness assessment from the district website and keeping a copy here at the house on 1200 Oakwood so I can bring it on my Sunday visits. We sit in her little recliner and I walk her through the nutrition and physical activity sections, and I point out what Ellie and Jake actually get for lunch so she doesn’t imagine them living on chips.

On top of that, I’ve been printing the 18-page Local School Wellness Policy at the admin center after my monthly care-plan meetings with her neurologist at Abbott Northwestern. The website is too confusing for her now, so I just bring the hard copy to Brookside and we look at the parts about sodium and heart health. It makes her feel like she still understands her grandkids’ world.

Work is…a lot. We keep saying we have “broad stakeholder engagement,” but honestly it’s mostly the same central office folks who sit on every committee with me. I feel guilty writing “community members and parents” when half the time it’s just Maria Gonzalez dropping in between PTO things.

Anyway, how are things at St. Luke’s? Next time you’re down here, maybe you can come with me to see Karen; she perks up when it’s not just me talking.

Love,
Mel

Dan,

I’m hiding in the bedroom for ten minutes before I go break up round three of “whose turn is it for the dishwasher.” Couple of things I need you in the loop on.

I finally emailed Jake’s pediatrician from Maple Grove Family Clinic the link to the district’s triennial wellness assessment, plus a PDF copy. After that February 2024 visit where she raised the weight gain and prediabetes concerns, I want us all looking at the same nutrition standards when we go back for his three‑month follow-up. It’s one thing for me to say, “The school lunches aren’t that bad,” and another to show her exactly what we’re serving.

And yes, I am still being paranoid about work. Ever since that parent filed the harassment complaint against me back in October 2022 over the nutrition lessons at the middle‑high campus, I’ve been saving timestamped PDFs of each assessment on my personal laptop here at 1200 Oakwood. HR said the case was closed, but I don’t trust that it won’t resurface if someone gets mad about energy drinks again.

Speaking of trust, remember when Xfinity shut off our internet last winter after we fell three months behind, right after your layoff from the Minneapolis healthcare IT job in November 2023? I had to print our own copy of the wellness policy at the district office reception desk so I wouldn’t burn through my phone hotspot. I’ve started telling other families in the same boat that they can just call the main line and have it mailed instead of pretending everyone can download PDFs.

Okay, back to the dishes war. Save me some tea if you put the kettle on.

M

artifacts.content:
Title: Wellness Committee – uncomfortable realities (for my eyes only)

Date: 2024-10-02

Jotting this down before I forget, because I need to be honest with myself somewhere.

We’ve been talking a lot about “public involvement,” but the truth is the last two wellness committee meetings have had zero parents or community members in the room. It’s been administrators, Carlos, Jenna, Patrick, and Mark, and that’s it. When the parent rep and the clinic liaison stopped showing up last year, I never carved out time to recruit replacements, and now it’s become the new normal.

At the same time, I keep pulling in people informally around the edges. I met with Ellie’s therapist, Dr. Sarah Nguyen at Lakeside Counseling, on February 10, 2025 for about 45 minutes to talk through how the middle‑high’s homework and exam schedules are feeding her panic attacks and sleep issues. That conversation is shaping how I’m thinking about the mental wellness language in the policy way more than anything said in our official meetings.

I’ve also leaned on Mark Ellis beyond his “classified staff rep” title. During a January 7, 2025 walk‑through at the middle‑high, he admitted he sometimes skips his blood pressure meds because the $95 monthly copay under the district plan is too much. That hit me hard; our staff wellness blurbs about step challenges feel ridiculous when people can’t afford their prescriptions.

Need to figure out how to bridge this gap between the tidy committee narrative and the messy reality I’m actually seeing.

Event: Ellie – 504 planning meeting + wellness policy review

Date: 2024-05-10
Time: 10:30–11:30 AM
Location: Maple Grove Middle-High School – Counseling Office
Attendees: Me, Ellie, school counselor (Ms. Larson), possibly Patrick (PE), maybe Jenna (nurse)

Notes:
- Ask front office to set aside a printed copy of the district’s triennial wellness assessment from the main office. I want something concrete to point to when we talk about how the nutrition and physical activity expectations intersect with Ellie’s anxiety and disordered eating.
- Since last fall (2023), she’s been avoiding lunch and dreading PE. After the panic attack in the cafeteria in early September 2024, she basically refused to go back to school for a week. Having the hard copy of the Local School Wellness Policy that Ms. Larson mailed with the 504 paperwork was actually easier for her to look at than the online version.
- Make sure we discuss options for quieter lunch spaces, flexible PE participation, and how to coordinate with Dr. Nguyen at Lakeside.
- Bring my own annotated copy of the wellness policy that I keep in the locked file cabinet at the district office with the May 2024 OCR inquiry notes about whether our nutrition education is hitting free/reduced‑price kids differently. Don’t mention OCR in the meeting, but I want to be clear in my own head where the policy gives us room to move.

Personal reminder: breathe, listen more than I talk, and try to show up as mom first, director second.

artifacts.content:
Subject: Where we really are on wellness outreach

Hi [Superintendent First Name],

Before your check‑in with the state folks next week, I wanted to give you a candid snapshot of where things stand on the wellness policy work so you’re not blindsided.

When I started in this role, we actually skipped the last triennial wellness assessment cycle. I was still figuring out systems, and we simply couldn’t pull the data together in time. We’ve done a much better job this round, but there’s still catch‑up happening in the background.

Public notification is another soft spot. For long stretches, the “communication plan” has basically been a link buried on the district website and the policy posted under the board section. Only in the last year have we been more consistent about SchoolMessenger blasts and newsletter blurbs.

On the family side, I’ve seen firsthand how personal crises affect engagement. At the March 12, 2025 wellness committee meeting at the district office, only three parents from the Somali Parent Network came, even though we’d sent January reminders. One of our most active parents, Amina Hassan, told me privately that her 8th‑grade son is in a spring diversion program after a shoplifting incident at the Maple Grove Target, and she’s avoiding school events because she doesn’t want anyone talking about it. That kind of fear doesn’t show up in attendance spreadsheets.

I’m not sharing this to be alarmist, just to ground our talking points in reality. There’s a lot to be proud of, but we also have clear gaps we can name and work on.

Best,
Melissa

Title: “Wellness Committee Challenges” running list

Date: 2024-01-09

Just left another thinly attended evening meeting and need to get this out of my head.

We keep saying we want “other stakeholders” at the table, but last year when we invited a couple of parents and a representative from the pediatric clinic, they came once and then disappeared. Life happens, I know, but the truth is we never really followed up to recruit new people after that. So right now, “outside stakeholders” mostly means the same PTO officer who pops in when she can, plus my informal conversations with folks like Maria Gonzalez at her house near Elm Creek Park.

I spent about an hour with Maria on August 22, 2025, listening to her talk about how their family had to sign up for free/reduced meals after her husband’s layoff last spring. It completely changed how she sees the stigma kids feel when they can’t afford the à la carte items. That conversation has stuck with me more than any of our formal agenda items.

I’m also thinking about my own family as “stakeholders,” whether I like it or not. Karen used to come to our public forums at the district office back in 2022–23, but when I called her on April 20, 2025 at Maple Grove Senior Living, she asked me to stop sending the long wellness emails. Her early‑stage Alzheimer’s has gotten worse over the past six months, and the flyers just confuse and upset her now.

Need to figure out a more humane way to think about involvement that accounts for people’s real lives.

Subject: Debrief from 9/19 presentation + random life stuff

Hey [Colleague’s First Name],

Catching my breath after the triennial wellness assessment presentation at Maple Grove Middle-High on 9/19/24 and thought you’d appreciate a debrief from someone in the same trenches.

I ended up soft‑pedaling the student participation data. After the October 3, 2023 student focus group, Ellie’s friend group (including her classmate Ryan M.) told me they actively avoid our wellness nights because they feel branded as “the kids in therapy.” Last spring they were getting pulled out of class twice a week for small‑group anxiety counseling in the counseling office, and now anything with “wellness” on it feels like a spotlight to them. I can’t exactly put that on a slide.

We also talked a lot about outreach strategies, but honestly, for a long time our “public notification” was just a lonely link on the district website. Only recently have we started layering in more direct communication to families and community partners.

On the home front, Mom (Maggie) is doing okay post‑stroke, but still struggles with online documents, so every three years I just mail her a printed copy of the triennial assessment from the Oakwood Drive office instead of asking her to download it in Bloomington. Ellie is juggling JV soccer, honors English, and panic about everything; Jake is thriving in robotics and somehow always covered in crumbs despite all my nutrition preaching.

If you ever want to trade sample agendas or just vent, I’m here. Sometimes it feels like we’re all reinventing the same wheel in different suburbs.

Take care,
Melissa

She can’t navigate the website anymore, but she still wants to talk about what Ellie and Jake are eating.

Sometimes it feels like my whole life is translating systems for people—families, staff, my own parents and in‑laws.

Love,
Mel

Subject: Jake’s suspension & tonight

Hey Daniel,

I just got out of a meeting with Mr. Kline about Jake’s three-day suspension for vaping in the boys’ bathroom at the Maple Grove Middle-High School Campus. The formal paperwork is brutal to read as a parent, even though I sign off on this kind of thing all the time in my Director of Student Wellness & Compliance role.

I asked Mr. Kline to tuck a printed copy of the Local School Wellness Policy in with Jake’s discipline packet so it’s all coming home together. The policy is technically accessible online at https://www.maplegroveusd.org/board/policies/lswp, but I don’t want this to feel like just another link he can ignore. Having the hard copy in front of us at the kitchen table at 1200 Oakwood Drive will, I hope, make it more concrete when we walk through the tobacco and substance-use sections with him.

Can you be home by 6:30 so we can eat quickly and then sit down with Jake for a real conversation? I’m thinking we frame it as: (1) health impacts, (2) school consequences—this three-day suspension in February 2024 can’t become a pattern—and (3) what support he needs so this doesn’t happen again.

I’m trying to keep my “district hat” off and just be Mom, but it’s hard not to see the policy language in my head. Let’s tag-team this so he hears both of us clearly.

– Melissa

From: melissa.hart@maplegroveusd.org
To: emily.olson@email.com
Date: April 19, 2025 7:38 AM
Subject: Thank you for yesterday’s meeting

Em,

I just wanted to say thank you again for joining the wellness committee meeting at the Maple Grove Unified School District office yesterday, April 18, 2025. Having you there as an informal community health voice—rather than just “my younger sister, Emily Olson”—made a real difference. When you shared that de-identified case from St. Luke’s in Duluth about the 13-year-old whose A1C climbed above 10% over six months because of daily energy drinks and the choices in his school’s vending machines, you could feel the room go quiet. It was exactly the kind of concrete example we needed to push the group toward tighter beverage guidelines.

I’ve also been thinking about how other family voices have helped shape this work. Remember how Karen Hart has now come to two of our wellness committee meetings at the Maple Grove Middle-High School Campus—one in March 2025 and one in May 2025? As a resident at Brookside Assisted Living in Plymouth, she’s been living with that low-sodium cardiac diet since her mild stroke back in November 2024, and her comments about how menu patterns affect people managing hypertension and early-stage kidney disease really landed with Carlos and Patrick.

Between your adolescent diabetes perspective and Karen’s post-stroke, low-sodium reality, I feel like we’re finally grounding the wellness policy in real lives instead of abstract guidelines.

Love,
Melissa

From: Jenna Lee <jlee@maplegroveusd.org>
To: Melissa Hart <mhart@maplegroveusd.org>
Date: April 9, 2025, 2:41 PM
Subject: Re: Clarifying dates for wellness reporting section

Melissa,

Circling back on the dates question from yesterday’s meeting.

For the “reporting timeframe month year to month year” line, I think it will be least confusing if we explicitly state the window that matches the bulk of our data tables: **October 2020 through September 2023**. That’s what I used for the BMI, fitness, and chronic condition trend graphs. Anything outside that range (like the early hydration campaign work) we can mention as context, but I wouldn’t expand the official timeframe just to capture a few pilot activities.

You also asked how to reference where families can actually see the triennial assessment. The full triennial assessment results are posted on the Maple Grove Unified School District website at https://www.maplegroveusd.org/departments/wellness/triennial-assessment, but because Karen Hart’s dementia has progressed since her fall in March 2023 at the Plymouth assisted living facility, I know you’ve been keeping a printed copy at your home at 1200 Oakwood Drive so you can go over the nutrition and physical activity sections with her during Sunday visits.

On “other stakeholders,” I agree with your note that we tried inviting a few parents and a local pediatric clinic representative last year, but after the first meeting they stopped coming and we haven’t followed up to recruit replacements, so there really aren’t any active outside stakeholders right now. If you want, I can frame that gently in the narrative so it doesn’t sound like we’re ignoring community voices.

Let me know if you decide to stick with October 2020 – September 2023 or change it again before we finalize.

Jenna

From: Melissa Hart <mhart@maplegroveusd.org>
To: Superintendent’s Cabinet <cabinet@maplegroveusd.org>
Date: May 20, 2025, 8:02 PM
Subject: Executive summary – wellness triennial assessment timeframe

Colleagues,

Attached is the near‑final executive summary for our wellness triennial assessment. Before we post, I need confirmation that you’re comfortable with the way I’ve framed the dates.

For clarity, I’ve described our review as covering **the three school years from September 2020 through June 2023**. That language seems to make the most sense to families and matches the way principals talk about initiatives (“since the 2020–21 school year,” etc.). The data tables occasionally dip into early fall 2023 where Jenna had complete information, but the narrative clearly labels those as “preliminary.”

To support transparency, I’ve reiterated that the triennial assessment results can be viewed on the Maple Grove Unified School District wellness page at https://www.maplegroveusd.org/departments/wellness/triennial-assessment, and for parents who can’t access the internet we’ll mail printed copies from the District Office at 1200 Oakwood Drive, Maple Grove, MN 55369. I’ve already arranged with my mother, Margaret Olson, to mail her a copy every three years because after her mild stroke in April 2021 in Bloomington she’s had trouble using her laptop and gets overwhelmed by online documents.

I’ve also added a short paragraph on “other stakeholders,” noting that my sister, Emily Olson, joined the April 18, 2025 wellness committee meeting at the district office as an informal community health voice.

Unless there are strong objections to the “September 2020 through June 2023” phrasing for the reporting timeframe, I’ll proceed with layout and web posting on Friday.

Best,
Melissa
